segments.text: Good morning, everyone.
Morning, morning, morning, mayor.

Thank you very much for coming in.

and, um, so we'll, we'll, we'll give it over to you
for a few minutes to, uh, to make some,
any introductory comments and then look forward to the
free wide ranging conversation.
Thank. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thanks for inviting us.
And just introduce, uh, to me, right, CFO,
Jason Dale, uh, Dale Wilson.
Dale's our chief customer and, uh, growth officer.
David Gordon looks after our metros
and capital programs in Duncan to our left, uh, looks
after her own business.
So, um, apologies from our board chair
because neither the acting chair
or the current acting deputy chair could be here today.
Uh, Sue Tyndall has been appointed as Key Rail's
chair starting one July,
and we look forward to Introduc to her next time.
But we are here. Um, I know my marks need to be, uh, brief,
so I'll get into it pretty quick.
Um, key rail is driving performance and going after growth,
and we've gotta focus in three key areas, safety,
performance, value, and briefly I'll go through those.
We're making a significant investment in our safety culture.
We're invested in training, technology and leadership,
and have an intense focus on reducing critical risks
and catastrophic risks.
We've had one serious harm injury
so far this financial year, compared
with sixth the previous year.
Our near misses that could result in a serious injury
or fatality have reduced 34%,
uh, compared to the previous year.
Uh, however, we still have a lot to do
to reduce the incident of critical risks,
which are still occurring.
Uh, and that is a core focus of management.
All injuries have reduced by 12% in the last 12 months,
and over 20% over the last two years.
And about 50 to 55%
of our injuries are slips, trips, and falls.
So a core focus on critical risk. Let's move to performance.
We're aiming to become customers first choice
for moving freight and people.
We have a strong customer led strategy for growth,
but this requires us to improve our reliability,
customer service, labor,
and asset productivity to ensure our pricing is competitive.
That's how the key rail above rail business,
or what we call our services business, will be sustainable.
And the goal is to have it standing on its own two feet.
Uh, after a long era of managed decline,
there's been 10 billion of investments since 2019
to help us build the platform for growth.
It's taking time to realize the benefits of that investment.
For example, even though funding
for our new Reliable Loco fleet was announced in budget
2021, the first two Locos are not yet in service
that we we're doing commissioning,
and they'll be in service in the next couple of months.
The next couple of years, we will see a number
of our assets come into service.
This streamlined fleet will help us lift reliability,
but we're already starting to make strides.
Uh, we call our fleet reliability dfo,
which is delivery in full on time for our customers.
It's the number one criteria when you're in the transport
game, that's your increased, your 86%,
and for our larger bulk and container traffic to
and from ports, it's now over 95% For
customers such as fontera.
Uh, the on-time performance from our premium freight service
is 90% in gradually lifting.
Our reliability for the inter island has been a standout.
At the end of quarter three was 98%.
In May, it was a hundred.
And over the last summer peak,
it was over a hundred percent.
I think that compares considerably against percent.
Hundred percent. It was a hundred percent.
I think that compares very well against
global airline businesses.
We've seen a, we've seen a strong improvement from
that from our customer service ratings.
Freight revenue, uh, for the last year, uh,
against tough economic conditions is in line with last year.
Despite the significant headwinds
and the closure of Windstone pulp,
which was our fourth largest freight customer,
our revenue from our scenic train services is
22% up on last year.
And we are on track to deliver our SCI full operating
surplus target for 110 million.
Uh, impact that will be impacted by the cost of redundancies
for our theory, which we are working through.
Now, let's turn quickly to value for money.
You'll know we've embarked on a
transformation of our business and work.
We're working hard to control costs, lift asset utilization,
lift labor productivity.
Our external costs, excluding our infrastructure
projects are down 3%.
Overall head count is down 7% over the past year.
There's very strange to our transformation plan.
And one is, and,
and we, what we're doing is we're taking hundreds
of ideas from a people to create savings and grow revenue.
Quick example, if you have two locomotives running together
for a heavy train, um, there's only a locomotive engineer
or a train driver in the front one.
So when the loco's riddling the train driver, the alley has
to get down from the first loco,
climb into the following loco and switch it off
and switch it on again when it's time to move.
Now, an idea from our frontline people was
that we are now doing that remotely,
and by using it, we estimate we'll save 1.2 million
liters of fuel a year.
That's just one of the initiatives from our people.
But in the last nine months of 25,
our transformation initiatives have delivered 37 million
of savings and margin improvement.

infrastructure business.

and uh, Christmas period.

day to improve the network.

However, we're getting results in Auckland.
Our infrastructure liability for metro services is at 98.9%.
Punctuality, arriving within five minutes
of the expected times is 95%.
For Kiwi Rail on Wellington, our reliability is 99.6%.
That means of 104,000 services that was scheduled
between one July last year
and Monday this week, 0.4% were canceled
because of key rail.

but it's 82% when all factors not just key rail accounted.
'cause of course, we do not operate
the trains in other city.

around congestion, pricing, et cetera.
So as we, as we start to look at road pricing
and rail pricing, we can start to think about
how we price different parts of the network.
Yeah. Yeah. So I, I mean,
I guess I totally understand like the public benefit
of investing in the rail line,
but is there a risk that the way it's currently working
that, you know, new Zealanders might be subsidizing private
business interests, like
for an off season coal mine on the west coast?
Um, rather than investing in the parts of the network
that have the most public benefit,
Um, look, we'll have to go through, through line by line,
but obviously through the rail review, um,
there's a track access fee methodology set.
Uh, we've gone through
and done a lot of work on our network economics
and look at each part, part of the network as a state
and enterprise as SOE,
we have a commercial mindset focus on that.
So, um, our job is to make sure that, um,
we're pricing, um, the product for the customer,
um, at the level where we can make money, that there is a,
there's a pathway to the tuck,
and we are not got, we can't do it by ourselves.
It's got to be part of what's happening
with road user charges is important.
What's happening with, with Tuck is important,
but we have an upward trajectory to make sure
that there is more user pays from our network.
Is, is the philosophy of thing, which we're trying to take.
Yeah. I, I absolutely focused on,
on value return across the network in terms
of commercial recovery as well.
So that's there. But, um, you know,
across the country there's different levels of activity
and return on that as well.
Um, and as there is in roads.
So it's not unique to just rail,
it's the same challenge in roads in terms of circumstances,
which comes back to what's the, the right pricing
for the transport system as on.
Yeah. Oh, that's true. Okay. Look, we are at time.
Um, thank you very much.
It would've been a great, I think, think, I mean,
I had half my questions still to go.
I'm sure that that's true for some of the others as well.
So, um, more time at some later stage would be good.
Can I just, um, say you will be aware, we're doing a,
an inquiry into the mari ports
and maritime sector, which also includes the relationship
with hinterlands and the transport networks, et cetera,
the service center lands.
I know that some of your customers are very, very keen
to submit, you know, forestry companies
and um, uh, you know, uh, agricultural
or cultural companies as well.
Mm-hmm. And we'd love to get your views on, you know,
how it best works, how the inland ports work,
the connections to ports work best, any opportunities
that there are, any challenges that there are.
Um, we've, we've asked for,
we've invited initial submissions by the 12th, um, of July.
Uh, if we don't hear from you,
we'll probably reach out anyway,
but it'll be good to, you know, have your initial thoughts.
It'll be great to help us with our, with our thinking.
Okay, sure. We'll, we'll, we'll, we'll pick that up. Yeah.
Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
That, that comes from, thank you.
